data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0822 GMT - The reaction on markets to U.S. tariffs on trade partners such as Canada and Switzerland has been relatively muted due to the fact that trade agreements have been sealed with Japan and the European Union, IG analysts say in a note. "Markets were relatively calm as key deals with Japan and the EU were already secured, and talks with China and Mexico continue," they say. The pan European Stoxx 600 index declines 1.1% to 539.84 points. Switzerland's SMI index falls 0.8%. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0819 GMT - Samsung Electronics could report a strong rebound in 3Q operating profit on better sales and profitability from its memory-chip segment, Nomura analysts led by C.W. Chung write in a research note. The South Korean technology giant's operating profit is forecast to jump 87% on quarter for the July-September period, they reckon. The company's high-bandwidth-memory shipments is forecast to surge 90% on quarter in 3Q, following a 28% rise in 2Q. DRAM prices are expected to rise by more than 10% on quarter in 3Q, they add. Nomura expects Samsung's return on equity to rise to 11.4% in 2026 from 7.6% in 2025. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0816 GMT - Apple shares rise premarket after the iPhone maker reported better-than-expected sales for its fiscal third quarter. Apple stock closed 0.7% lower at $207.57 Thursday ahead of results. Shares are up 2% at $211.79 Friday premarket. The tech giant reported $94.04 billion in sales for the three months ended June 28, above a FactSet forecast of $89.60 billion. The iPhone accounted for the lion's share of sales, contributing $44.58 billion. Analysts polled by FactSet had forecast $40.05 billion in iPhone sales. Net profit of $23.43 billion also beat consensus of $21.67 billion. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0802 GMT - Oil prices are up significantly on week, with Brent and WTI 6% and 6.3% higher, respectively. With a better growth backdrop from positive economic data releases and concerns over tightening Russian supply in July, Brent crude posted its best month since September 2023, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. Oil prices rallied late in the month after President Trump threatened new sanctions or secondary tariffs against importers of Russian oil, with a deadline of Aug. 8, analysts write. U.S. trade policy also appeared more successful over July, with a raft of trade deals signed late in the month, raising economic hopes and optimism for oil demand. In early morning trade, Brent crude is flat at $71.69 a barrel and WTI is down 0.1% at $69.22 a barrel. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0802 GMT - IAG's 250 million-euro EBIT beat in 2Q is enough to drive upgrades of 5% to 6%, RBC Capital Markets analyst Ruairi Cullinane says in a note. The airline group lowered its total fuel cost guidance for the year by 5.3% to 7.1 billion euros, which could make consensus estimates rise by even more, he says. The company is 57% booked for the second half with revenue in line with last year, compared with 29% booked for the 2H last year. "We think IAG has a stronger outlook than given credit for, given a supportive capacity backdrop on the Atlantic, pricing potential from BA product measures, and given scope for business travel recovery to step up further," he says. Shares are up 1.3% at 385.50 pence. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0758 GMT - Macau's gaming industry is likely to maintain solid momentum, according to Citi analysts in a research note. Gross gaming revenue in July beat Citi's estimates and market consensus. Looking ahead, Eason Chan's concert series at the Galaxy Arena over the next two weekends is likely to boost gross gaming revenue similarly to Jacky Cheung's in June and July, Citi says. Citi raises its August gross gaming revenue forecast to 21.5 billion Macanese Pataca from 20.0 billion Macanese Pataca, implying on-year growth of 9%. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0750 GMT - The dollar should gain further if U.S. jobs data for July is strong but any rise is likely to be limited, MUFG's Derek Halpenny says in a note. The data is due at 1230 GMT. Good news on the U.S. economy is already largely reflected in the price of the dollar, which rose by 3.19% against a basket of currencies in July, its best month since April 2022. "The [dollar's] strong gain in July does suggest positive news is better priced, which will contain the scope for dollar strength today," he says. Still, a bigger-than-expected rise in nonfarm payrolls could remove the prospect of an interest-cut in September, he says. The DXY dollar index edges up 0.1% to 100.022, having hit a two-month high of 100.145 earlier. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0741 GMT - European stocks fall sharply in early trade, led by losses in the healthcare and insurance sectors, after President Trump signed an executive order Thursday night significantly raising tariffs on scores of nations. Trump also sent letters to 17 big pharmaceutical companies asking them to lower costs of drugs for U.S. consumers. The Stoxx Europe 600 falls 1%, with the insurance and healthcare sector indexes down 1.6% and 1.5%, respectively. Germany's DAX, France's CAC 40 and Italy's FTSE MIB all drop more than 1%. Markets are closed in Switzerland--which will be hit with a 39% tariff, Trump's order said--due to a local holiday. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0732 GMT - Maruti Suzuki India appears optimistic on the demand outlook for 2Q FY 2026, Elara Securities (India) analysts say in a research report. The automaker sees demand picking up in 2Q, led by the early festival season, good monsoon and robust rural demand, the analysts note. The Indian company's two new launches within sports utility vehicles are on track, while dealer inventory is normalized at 33 days as of end-1Q. The key catalyst for the stock is a recovery in domestic demand in India, which will be keenly monitored by the brokerage during the festive season. The brokerage maintains the stock's accumulate rating, but raises the target price to INR14,279.00 from INR13,961.00 to reflect valuation roll-forward. Shares are 1.9% lower at INR12,393.90. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0728 GMT - Shares of European pharmaceutical companies fell in early trade after U.S. President Trump sent out letters Thursday asking them to cut the costs of drugs for U.S. customers to the lowest offered in other developed nations.Shares of GSK, AstraZeneca, Novo Nordisk, and Sanofi were down 1.7%, 2.8%, 4.2% and 1.2%, respectively. The Stoxx Europe 600 healthcare index was down 1.8%. The President said he would use "every tool in our arsenal" to protect American families from high prices if companies didn't act voluntarily. (ian.walker@wsj.com.)

0714 GMT - South Korea's benchmark Kospi fell 3.9% to close at 3119.41, posting its biggest daily decline since April. 7. The selloff was led by heavy institutional and foreign selling. Investor sentiment was weighed by the new left-leaning Seoul government's tax-increase plan, which includes raising the stock transaction tax to 0.2% from 0.15%, according to NH Investment & Securities market analyst Kim Jong-youn. Chip and defense stocks led the retreat. Index heavyweight Samsung Electronics fell 3.5%. Memory-chip maker SK Hynix dropped 5.7%, snapping a three-session winning streak. Ammunition manufacturer Poongsan slumped 16%. Self-propelled howitzer and aircraft engine maker Hanwha Aerospace slid 5.7%.(kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0707 GMT - London Stock Exchange Group's progress has been overlooked, JP Morgan Cazenove analysts write in a note. The negative market reaction to the company's recent performance was overdone, they add. The analysts recognize concerns over competition and hurdles in Data & Analytics unit. However, they believe LSEG's solid constant-currency growth and improved operating leverage--including the lifted Ebitda margin guidance--have been overlooked. LSEG continues to be a unique asset in the European financial sector, with diversified revenue stream across industries that are benefiting from long-term growth trends, they say. The company's strong cash generation further enhance its appeal, they add. Shares are up 0.8% at 9,330 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Bitcoin Falls to Three-Week Low After Tariff News, Ahead of U.S. Data — Market Talk
data.description:
0821 GMT - Bitcoin falls to its lowest level in three weeks as stock markets fall after President Trump announced steeper tariffs against dozens of countries, even as he delayed the effective date by a week. Markets are particularly cautious ahead of key U.S. jobs data due at 1230 GMT, which could further dampen prospects of another interest-rate cut in September. Bitcoin extends falls after recently dropping below chart support around $117,500, analysts say. Bitcoin falls 1.7% to $114,568, having hit a low of $114,330 overnight, LSEG data show. The cryptocurrency drops well away from the record high of $123.153 hit in mid-July. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0810 GMT - Gold futures slip, and are on track to end the week lower on easing safe-haven demand. Futures are down 0.3% at $3,337.70 a troy ounce. The precious metal has slid in recent days as market turns risk-on, BMI analysts say in a note. Strong economic data out of the U.S., as well as the U.S.-European Union trade agreement, saw the U.S. dollar's strength surge, heaping downward pressure on gold, BMI says. A stronger dollar makes it more expensive for international purchasers to buy bullion and directly competes with gold's safe-haven characteristics. Still, BMI expects around 50 basis points of U.S. interest-rate cuts this year, which should support non-interest bearing bullion. With further tariff announcements also likely, gold prices should remain elevated, BMI adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0807 GMT - Bank Indonesia is expected to leave its benchmark rate unchanged at the August policy meeting, RHB's Wong Xian Yong says in a note. Any future easing will depend on rupiah stability and global sentiment, the economist writes. Demand-driven inflation will likely be subdued this quarter, with a pickup expected only during the year-end holiday season, he adds. RHB forecasts a final rate cut in September but notes that the timing will be data-dependent amid domestic financial-market stability and the broader external risk environment. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0750 GMT - The dollar should gain further if U.S. jobs data for July is strong but any rise is likely to be limited, MUFG's Derek Halpenny says in a note. The data is due at 1230 GMT. Good news on the U.S. economy is already largely reflected in the price of the dollar, which rose by 3.19% against a basket of currencies in July, its best month since April 2022. "The [dollar's] strong gain in July does suggest positive news is better priced, which will contain the scope for dollar strength today," he says. Still, a bigger-than-expected rise in nonfarm payrolls could remove the prospect of an interest-cut in September, he says. The DXY dollar index edges up 0.1% to 100.022, having hit a two-month high of 100.145 earlier. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0737 GMT - Taiwan's exports growth could trend down noticeably in coming months after likely having peaked in May, Citi analyst Adrienne Lui says in a research note. Exports could suffer from paybacks from previous frontloading and a high comparison base from August onwards, the analyst says. Taiwan's exporters may have earned their full year's business in 1H, while 2H demand could correct as consumer electronics are becoming well stocked for U.S. end buyers for the rest of the year, she says. However, AI-related exports could still have a long demand pipeline, she adds. Despite the headwinds, Citi upgrades its 2025 Taiwan GDP growth forecast to 3.5% after a robust 2Q. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0735 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds rise, along with their eurozone and U.S. counterparts, ahead of key U.S. jobs data for July at 1230 GMT. Recent U.S. data have pointed to a robust U.S. economy. Analysts say strong jobs data could add to speculation that the Federal Reserve might not cut interest rates in the coming months. Weak data, however, would increase prospects of a rate reduction in September. Meanwhile in the U.K., data from Nationwide showed house prices rose by 0.6% in July after falling in June. The 10-year gilt yields climb 6 basis points to last trade at 4.628%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0725 GMT - Indonesia's trade surplus is expected to soften this year, UOB economists write in a research note. The country posted a trade surplus in June driven primarily by robust non-oil and gas exports. However, the trade surplus could moderate going forward. Escalating uncertainties, particularly those stemming from the U.S. reciprocal tariffs, are expected to weigh on external demand and export growth, the economists say. Meanwhile, imports may rise on the back of Indonesia's trade negotiations with the U.S. and increased inflows of Chinese goods, they add. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0724 GMT - The dollar has the potential to rise even further if U.S. nonfarm payrolls data at 1230 GMT are strong, ING's Francesco Pesole says in a note. The U.S. currency hit a two-month high against a basket of currencies overnight and Friday's jobs data "presents the best chance for the dollar to take an extra leap higher," he says. Nonfarm payrolls are expected to rise by 100,000 in July, according to the consensus in a WSJ poll. ING expects a bigger rise of 115,000, while there is talk in the market of an even greater gain of around 120,000, Pesole says. The DXY dollar index is last steady at 100.00, having hit 100.145 earlier. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0708 GMT - Chinese exports are likely to remain steady for now, according to Citi economists in a research note. Bilateral trade between the U.S. and China held up well despite the underwhelming bilateral trade talks in Stockholm, Citi points out. Overall cargo throughout was also steady toward the month-end amid various weather events, they say. The analysts are "comfortable" with their forecast of around 8% year-on-year exports growth for July. If the truce continues to hold, China and the rest of the world's tariff differential will likely narrow after the modified reciprocal tariffs on other economies become effective later this month, they note.(tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0708 GMT - The narrative about U.S. exceptionalism was challenged in the first half of 2025 but it gained renewed momentum in July, resulting in the dollar's best month since April 2022, Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid and Peter Sidorov say in a note. The dollar gained 3.19% against a basket of currencies last month. The DXY dollar index last trades steady at 99.979, having hit a two-month high of 100.145 in overnight trade. The DXY traded at 96.875 at the end of June. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0703 GMT - Improving risk sentiment is likely to weigh on German Bunds, ING's Michiel Tukker says in a note. During months of trade tensions, Bunds reaffirmed their status as safe havens. However, now that trade deals being struck, "we may see lower demand going forward," the senior European rates strategist says. Just after the German spending announcement, the 10-year Bund yield traded some 15 basis points above the swap, a level that is unlikely to be reached again any time soon, he says. The 10-year German Bund-swap spread is currently close to zero. The yield on the 10-year Bund rises 3.5 basis points to last trade at 2.726%, according to LSEG. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0655 GMT - Asean market watchers will keep a close eye on sector-specific tariffs, RHB Bank group chief economist and head of market research Barnabas Gan says in a report. These tariffs pose downside risks for regional countries, especially those with export-heavy industries, Gan writes. "Malaysia and Singapore appear most exposed to potential semiconductor-related tariffs, while pharmaceutical-related measures would likely weigh heavily on Singapore and Thailand." Thailand's automotive sector and Indonesia's electric-vehicle battery segment could also be hurt by any new levies targeting the broader auto value chain, he says. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Steel Holds Sharp Pullback
data.description:
Steel futures in China plunged to CNY 3,190 per tonne from the seven-month high of CNY 3,345 on July 29th as markets reconsidered the scale of Chinese fiscal support against the slowdown in consumption and the prolonged property crisis.

Beijing noted it will implement expansionary fiscal and monetary policy to shore up spending but refrained from signaling large-scale stimulus measures.

Additionally, tariffs on major economies by the US drove ArcelorMittal to lower its guidance and forecast less steel consumption outside of China.

Also on the tariff front, pig iron and ferro-alloys were exempted from US tariffs on Brazil, a key supplier, to ease the steep sectoral tariffs on steel.

Still, the outlook of lower supply and robust infrastructure spending maintained steel futures 5% higher in the third quarter.

Chinese policymakers vowed to erase overcapacity in construction inputs, with Baosteel expecting domestic output to fall by over 50 million tonnes this year due to policy bounds.

data.title: Russian pipeline gas exports to Europe rose 37% m/m in July, data shows
data.description:
Russian energy giant Gazprom's average daily natural gas supplies to Europe increased by 37% in July from a month earlier when maintenance work reduced them, Reuters calculations showed on Friday.

Turkey and the TurkStream undersea pipeline is the only transit route left for Russian gas to Europe after Ukraine chose not to extend a five-year transit deal with Moscow when it expired on January 1.

Calculations based on data from European gas transmission group Entsog showed that Russian gas exports via the TurkStream pipeline rose to 51.5 million cubic metres (mcm) per day in July from 37.6 mcm per day in June.

That was 4.7% more than July 2024 when they stood at 49.2 mcm.

Total Russian gas supplies to Europe via TurkStream stood at around 9.93 billion cubic metres (bcm) in the first six months of this year, compared to 9.3 bcm during the same period a year earlier, according to Reuters calculations.

Gazprom’s exports to Europe in January-July last year amounted to 18.3 bcm, a figure which included volumes transited through Ukraine.

The company, which has not published its own monthly statistics since the start of 2023, did not respond to a request for comment.

Russia supplied about 63.8 bcm of gas to Europe by various routes in 2022, Gazprom data and Reuters calculations show.

That plummeted by 55.6% to 28.3 bcm in 2024, but increased to around 32 bcm in 2024.

At their peak in 2018-2019, annual gas flows to Europe reached between 175 and 180 bcm.

data.title: Hungary Factory Activity Rebounds in July
data.description:
The HALPIM Manufacturing PMI rose to 50.7 in July 2025 from an upwardly revised 49 in the previous month.

Most sub-indices showed improvement, with eight of them now in the recovery range.

Notably, the sub-index for new orders jumped by 5.2 points, while production rose by 1.6 points.

The employment index also rose this month, signaling a recovery after two consecutive months of contraction.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0816 GMT - Apple shares rise premarket after the iPhone maker reported better-than-expected sales for its fiscal third quarter. Apple stock closed 0.7% lower at $207.57 Thursday ahead of results. Shares are up 2% at $211.79 Friday premarket. The tech giant reported $94.04 billion in sales for the three months ended June 28, above a FactSet forecast of $89.60 billion. The iPhone accounted for the lion's share of sales, contributing $44.58 billion. Analysts polled by FactSet had forecast $40.05 billion in iPhone sales. Net profit of $23.43 billion also beat consensus of $21.67 billion. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0655 GMT - Asean market watchers will keep a close eye on sector-specific tariffs, RHB Bank group chief economist and head of market research Barnabas Gan says in a report. These tariffs pose downside risks for regional countries, especially those with export-heavy industries, Gan writes. "Malaysia and Singapore appear most exposed to potential semiconductor-related tariffs, while pharmaceutical-related measures would likely weigh heavily on Singapore and Thailand." Thailand's automotive sector and Indonesia's electric-vehicle battery segment could also be hurt by any new levies targeting the broader auto value chain, he says. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0613 GMT - Kaynes Technology India's growth visibility remains high, Nomura analysts say in a research report. They note that the company maintained its revenue target of more than 45 billion rupees, with upside risk to margin guidance of 15.5% for FY 2026. The management expects its smart meter business to post revenue of 10 billion rupees to 12 billion rupees in FY 2026, with good traction from two-wheeler EVs. The integrated electronics manufacturing company also anticipates getting around 8% revenue from its aerospace business with new customers. Nomura raises the stock's target price to INR7,878.00 from INR7,183.00, with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are 2.7% higher at INR6,340.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0427 GMT - China's WuXi AppTec could see increased demand for its contract research, development and manufacturing services over the next few years, UOB Kay Hian analysts say in a note. They expect surging biopharmaceutical investment to drive demand, leading to earnings growth. WuXi could improve profit margins from 2H, given higher capacity utilization and the sale of its loss-making cell-and-gene therapy business. The analysts raise their 2025-2027 revenue compound annual growth rate estimate to 14.8%, citing stronger-than-expected results from its contract drug-development segment. They also forecast a 22.1% adjusted earnings CAGR over 2025-2027. UOB KH raises its rating to buy from hold with a higher target price of HK$146.00 from HK$70.00 on its H-shares. Shares are 2.2% lower atHK$103.40. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0235 GMT - Taiwan's robust 2Q economic expansion prompts ANZ to raise its GDP growth forecast for the economy to 6.2% in 2025 and 3.5% in 2026. The updated forecast is conservative, however, as it assumes flat sequential growth in 3Q and 4Q, according to ANZ's Bansi Madhavani. Taiwan's exports could rise 28% in 2025, adding 4.1 percentage points to headline GDP growth, the economist says in a note. Taiwan's economic growth is "a proxy for the global digital economy, which is experiencing a structural upturn thanks to the increasing demand for high-precision chips." The central bank will likely stand pat given the island's robust economy and easing inflation, she adds. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0221 GMT - Malaysia's economic plan targeting 4.5%-5.5% GDP growth annually from 2026 to 2030 appears achievable given recent strong expansion driven by private consumption, CIMB Securities analysts say. But the government's move to cut reliance on foreign workers could raise costs for labor-intensive sectors, Ivy Ng Lee Fang and Lim Yue Jia write in a note. The construction sector stands to benefit from development spending, while affordable housing and clean energy are key focus areas, they add. As Malaysia boosts its role in global semiconductor supply chains, CIMB sees local beneficiaries in IC design and automated test equipment, especially those aligned with AI and advanced packaging. Labor shortages could weigh on segments such as electronics manufacturing services and outsourced semiconductor assembly and testing. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0215 GMT - Australian telecommunications providers' near-term operating expenses could be lower than expected as they cut costs in customer support and lay off more staff, Macquarie analysts say. They wouldn't be surprised if Telstra makes more job cuts on top of its 550 recent redundancies, listing lay-offs as one of four potential catalysts for the stock. Mobile price rises, M&A activity and capital management are the other things they are looking out for ahead of Telstra's annual result announcement later this month. Macquarie has an outperform rating and A$5.19 target price on Telstra shares, which are down 0.5% at A$4.945. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2352 GMT - Life360's new bull at Citi sees the tracking-app developer expanding into financial services and wearables as it reaches beyond its core customer base. Initiating coverage of the stock with a buy rating, analyst Siraj Ahmed sees a partnership with other providers as the route for this expansion. He also anticipates increased customer engagement from the generation of member-specific discounts and offers, which will help its nascent ad-revenue venture. Ahmed tells clients in a note that Life360 should pass its target of 150 million active users in 2028. He puts a US$90 target price on Life360's U.S.-listed stock, and a A$46.20 target price on the Australia-listed stock, which is at A$40.16 ahead of the open. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2242 GMT [Dow Jones]--Gentrack loses a bull in Jefferies after a few recent contract tenders didn't go the software company's way. "In our view, the industry outlook remains a tailwind for Gentrack's outlook, but we also see increased competition," analyst Wei Sim says. Gentrack last week said it's no longer part of a process to replace an Australian customer's platform. Jefferies says the customer could well be Red Energy. But the bank says the gap created by the Australian customer loss "is likely to be more than offset over the medium term by new contract wins, similar to the situation with the loss of U.K. insolvent businesses in prior years." Jefferies downgrades Gentrack to hold, from buy, and cuts its price target by 21% to A$11.00/share. Gentrack ended Thursday at A$9.75. (david.winning@wsj.com)

2202 GMT - Consumers rushing to buy pricey electronics bumped sales up in the quarter ended in June. Apple's sales grew 10% during the quarter, and executives estimate one percentage point of that growth was driven by shoppers trying to buy ahead of tariffs. Because of that, Apple is expecting sales won't stay at that high level of growth, as it gave a full-year outlook with revenue percentage growth in the single digits. Apple anticipates the cost of tariffs will increase to $1.1 billion in the current quarter, up from $800 million the quarter before. The jump in costs is partly due to the fact that volume will be rise, executives say. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

2103 GMT - Apple's jump in 2Q sales was driven by people buying more iPhones and Mac computers. The tech giant posted a 13% increase in iPhone sales and a 15% climb in Mac sales. Those two categories offset 8% and 9% declines in revenue from iPads and wearables/home accessories. Its service segment, which includes entertainment and cloud storage, also grew 13%. Asia Pacific and Japan had the largest increase in sales, while the Americas and Europe (Apple's largest geographical markets) both had sales rise around 9%. The growth in sales comes as tariffs are expected to drive up prices. Shares rise 1.5% after hours. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

2040 GMT - Figma priced shares at $33 a piece and closed its first trading day at $115.50, for a 250% pop. That means the company had the highest first-day gain ever for an IPO sized at $500 million or more, according to Renaissance Capital, a provider of pre-IPO research and IPO-focused ETFs. Figma's debut is "a shot in the arm for the IPO market," said Matt Kennedy, senior strategist at Renaissance. "So I now feel more confident that we'll have a fairly robust calendar in the fall, and an even bigger 2026 than we expected," he added about the slate of future IPOs. "Hype and FOMO have returned to the IPO market!" Renaissance said in a post on X earlier Thursday. Only two other companies with public offerings of that size had first-day trading increases of greater than 150% in the research firm's 30 years of records-- those are Circle, which went public earlier this year, and Palm, which listed in March 2000. (yuliya.chernova@wsj.com)

Accent Group Limited and Accent Capital PLC Boards announce  the  resignation of Tom Miskell and the appointment of Nigel Wright to the role of Group Chair.

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0741 GMT - European stocks fall sharply in early trade, led by losses in the healthcare and insurance sectors, after President Trump signed an executive order Thursday night significantly raising tariffs on scores of nations. Trump also sent letters to 17 big pharmaceutical companies asking them to lower costs of drugs for U.S. consumers. The Stoxx Europe 600 falls 1%, with the insurance and healthcare sector indexes down 1.6% and 1.5%, respectively. Germany's DAX, France's CAC 40 and Italy's FTSE MIB all drop more than 1%. Markets are closed in Switzerland--which will be hit with a 39% tariff, Trump's order said--due to a local holiday. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0728 GMT - Shares of European pharmaceutical companies fell in early trade after U.S. President Trump sent out letters Thursday asking them to cut the costs of drugs for U.S. customers to the lowest offered in other developed nations.Shares of GSK, AstraZeneca, Novo Nordisk, and Sanofi were down 1.7%, 2.8%, 4.2% and 1.2%, respectively. The Stoxx Europe 600 healthcare index was down 1.8%. The President said he would use "every tool in our arsenal" to protect American families from high prices if companies didn't act voluntarily. (ian.walker@wsj.com.)

0451 GMT - The U.S.'s 19% tariff on Malaysian exports is slightly negative for the glove sector, as it will narrow Malaysia's pricing advantage, says TA Securities analyst Tan Kong Jin in a note. Customers are expected to continue resisting cost-sharing as oversupply persists, while Malaysian glove makers' plant utilization rates remain below optimal levels, he notes. Trade uncertainty also lingers, with ongoing U.S.-China tariff negotiations. Tan doesn't rule out target price cuts on Malaysian glove stocks if China, Malaysia's biggest competitor in the glove industry, secures a more favorable trade deal. TA Securities maintains an underweight rating on the sector as it expects global oversupply to last at least three more years. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0427 GMT - China's WuXi AppTec could see increased demand for its contract research, development and manufacturing services over the next few years, UOB Kay Hian analysts say in a note. They expect surging biopharmaceutical investment to drive demand, leading to earnings growth. WuXi could improve profit margins from 2H, given higher capacity utilization and the sale of its loss-making cell-and-gene therapy business. The analysts raise their 2025-2027 revenue compound annual growth rate estimate to 14.8%, citing stronger-than-expected results from its contract drug-development segment. They also forecast a 22.1% adjusted earnings CAGR over 2025-2027. UOB KH raises its rating to buy from hold with a higher target price of HK$146.00 from HK$70.00 on its H-shares. Shares are 2.2% lower atHK$103.40. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

1523 GMT - Moderna's decision to cut its workforce by 10% will help offset declining Covid-19 vaccine revenue, says Leerink Partners analyst Mani Foroohar. Moderna CEO Stephane Bancel said in a memo to employees that even after cutting back on research and manufacturing costs, the company needed to cut the jobs to reach its goal of reducing operating expenses by $1.4 billion to $1.7 billion by 2027. "Every effort was made to avoid affecting jobs," he said. Moderna, which grew rapidly when demand for Covid shots was much higher earlier this decade, will have fewer than 5,000 employees by the end of this year. Shares fall 4.7%. ( peter.loftus@wsj.com )

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0707 GMT - London Stock Exchange Group's progress has been overlooked, JP Morgan Cazenove analysts write in a note. The negative market reaction to the company's recent performance was overdone, they add. The analysts recognize concerns over competition and hurdles in Data & Analytics unit. However, they believe LSEG's solid constant-currency growth and improved operating leverage--including the lifted Ebitda margin guidance--have been overlooked. LSEG continues to be a unique asset in the European financial sector, with diversified revenue stream across industries that are benefiting from long-term growth trends, they say. The company's strong cash generation further enhance its appeal, they add. Shares are up 0.8% at 9,330 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0042 GMT - Judo Capital's achievement in hitting its annual gross-loan target elicits a sigh of relief from Morgans analyst Nathan Lead. He tells clients in a note that the Australian business lender's A$12.465 billion gross-loan balance at June 30 should give investors increased confidence. Judo had guided for A$12.4 billion-A$12.6 billion. He points out that Judo recorded its largest-ever monthly rise in loan growth in June, but cautions against reading too much into it due to the seasonality of drawdowns. It's also positive that term deposits grew by more than loans, even if the balance fell short of expectations. Morgans keeps an accumulate rating and A$1.75 target price on the stock. Shares are down 0.5% at A$1.5075. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1658 GMT - The U.S. housing market is cooling overall, Realtor.com says, but the pace and severity of the slowdown varies widely across regions. The southern and western U.S. are shifting decisively in favor of buyers, with rising inventory, deeper price cuts, and longer time on market. In contrast, conditions in the Northeast and Midwest remain much tighter. Active listings rose for the 21st straight month, homes took 7 days longer to sell than last year, and 33 of the 50 largest metros posted year-over-year price declines--including Austin, down 4.9%, Miami off 4.7%, and Chicago 4.4% lower. More than one in five sellers cut their asking price. Only 19 of the 50 largest U.S. metros currently have prices below July 2022, and all are located in the South and West. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1331 GMT - Schroders's results were at least in line with market expectations and in many areas much better, Panmure Liberum says in a research note. The U.K. asset manager posted net inflows and positive market movements for the first half, which lifted its assets under management to 670.6 billion pounds. "The market has been churlish in grasping the fundamental changes being undertaken at Schroders, both in respect of expected profits and also the multiple which is applied to estimated earnings; we see upside in both," the analysts write. They add that the group is running comfortably ahead of schedule to remove costs. Shares fall 0.5% to 388 pence and are up 20% year to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

1325 GMT - Just Group shareholders should be supportive being taken over at a very attractive premium, Jefferies says in a research note. The U.K. retirement-income products and services provider agreed to be bought by Brookfield Wealth Solutions for 2.4 billion pounds. "In our view, it is unlikely that Just Group's shareholders will achieve better value from either another strategic buyer, or as a separately listed company in the short-to-medium term," analyst Philip Kett writes. The 220 pence-a-share bid implies a 75% premium to Wednesday's closing price and a 29% premium to the stock's record high in April 2016, he adds. Shares soar 68% to 212 pence. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

1312 GMT - St. James's Place's better-than-expected inflows, funds under management and cash profits for the first half helped shares soar to their highest level since March 2023. There was a fear that pricing changes would overshadow these results, but the opposite happened, Panmure Liberum says in a research note after the U.K. wealth manager's results and provision release. "The level of business production is a clear reminder that pricing is not the be-all-and-end-all for clients that it can be for some in the stock market; advice and relationships matter and St Jimmy's leads the way," analysts write. Shares are up 9.3% at 1,278.5 pence. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

1137 GMT - Cigna Group reports fewer total pharmacy customers at the end of 2Q than the prior year, down 0.5% to 121.9 million. Total medical customers declined 5.2% to around 18 million. Behavioral care customers rose to about 23.9 million from 23.8 million and dental edged up to 18.4 million from 18.3 million. Cigna's customer relationship counts are closely watched as healthcare and insurance businesses navigate higher medical costs, with some cutting their forecasts, in part due to a shifting membership base. Cigna ticks up 2% premarket. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1055 GMT - Prudential PLC appears to have saved hundreds of millions of dollars by settling a legal dispute with a shareholder in one of its main Malaysian businesses, Jefferies says in a research note. The group unexpectedly announced it settled with Detik Ria, who had started proceedings in April and sought $833 million in unpaid dividends plus interest. The settlement shrank the cost to one-tenth of the original amount, saving Prudential at least $717 million--or 2% of its market capitalization--analysts write. "Given that this calculation does not factor in the potential interest that has now been forgone, and that the settlement de-risks both the stock and the use of the proceeds from the impending IPO of ICICI Prudential Asset Management, we are not surprised [the] stock has risen," they add. Shares in London rise 2% to 957.2 pence. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0900 GMT - London Stock Exchange Group's results offered no extra surprises beside the bigger share buyback, AJ Bell's Russ Mould says. The stock-exchange and financial-information company announced plans for a further 1 billion pound share buyback over the coming months. The stock still seems fairly valued based on earnings, Mould adds. This is an important point, even after the recent 20% drop from its peak, he says. Shares are down 4.6% at 95.90 pounds. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0842 GMT - London Stock Exchange Group's first-half performance is likely to lead to positive revisions to consensus estimates, JP Morgan Cazenove analysts write in a note. The stock-exchange and financial-information company delivered market-beating results and announced plans for a 1-billion-pound share buyback in the second half. The earnings beat, coupled with the larger-than-expected buyback, could lead to a low-to mid-single digit increase in consensus earnings per share, they say. However, the analysts also note a decline in annual subscription value, citing increased competition as a contributing factor. Shares are down 4.1% at 96.46 pounds. ( najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Engie Shares Drop After Earnings Hit by Lower Energy Prices, Volatility
data.description:
By Adam Whittaker

Shares in Engie fell after the French power utility's first-half earnings were dragged down by lower energy prices and market volatility.

In morning European trade, shares were down 3.8% to 18.88 euros, but remain up 23% over the year to date.

Engie said Friday that earnings before interest and taxes excluding nuclear power, a key company metric, fell 6.4% on an organic basis to 5.1 billion euros ($5.82 billion).

Earnings were slightly light but this was expected, J.P. Morgan analysts wrote in a note to clients. Normalizing energy prices and macroeconomic volatility dragged on its results but much of this was well understood by the market in advance, they added.

Engie said it took a hit of nearly 100 million euros hit from negative exchange-rate changes, driven mainly by the depreciation of the Brazilian real. Earnings were also hurt by the loss of income following the sale of stakes in assets in Morocco, Singapore and Pakistan. However, earnings were helped by higher temperatures in France, the company said.

Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization fell 5.5% on an organic basis to 8.3 billion euros, while revenue rose 2.9% on an organic basis to 38.1 billion euros.

For 2025, the company continues to expect recurring net profit between 4.4 billion and 5 billion euros and EBIT excluding earnings from nuclear power between 8 billion and 9 billion euros.

Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0127 GMT - Origin Energy's near-term spending will probably be larger when its part-owned Reedy Creek and Iron Bark gas projects are approved, according to Macquarie analysts. They flag that costs will be structurally higher in the coming three years as the Australia Pacific LNG venture starts replacing production. They tell clients in a note that they anticipate negative cash-flow impacts from fiscal 2027 onwards. Macquarie keeps a neutral rating and A$10.94 target price on the stock, which is up 0.5% at A$11.725. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0014 GMT - Oil edges higher in the early Asian session, supported by supply-disruption concerns. President Trump has given Russian President Putin an Aug. 8 deadline to reach a cease-fire with Ukraine, shortening a 50-day deadline he had set. The deadline for Russia to end its invasion and a full ceasefire has aided some worries over supply disruptions, says Janiv Shah, vice president at Rystad Energy, in an email. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are up 0.1% at $69.33/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are 0.1% higher at $71.80/bbl. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2252 GMT - Beach Energy's quarterly update had a sting in its tail for Euroz Hartleys. While Beach achieved FY 2025 guidance, it also signaled several negative developments. Among them: Faster-than-anticipated field decline in the Otway Basin, prolonged flooding impacts in the Cooper Basin, and a delay to first gas from the second stage of its Waitsia project. Euroz Hartleys cuts a forecast for annual production to 19.7 million BOE, from 23.0 million BOE. It also pares expectations for Ebitda, operating cash flow and dividends. Euroz Hartleys lowers its price target by 13%, to A$1.32/share, noting that drilling of the Beharra Springs Deep 3 gas well suggests the field in the Perth Basin has lower accessible reserves than previously thought. Beach fell 9.3% yesterday, to A$1.17. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1928 GMT - Oil futures snap a three-day winning streak with the approach of the U.S. tariff deadline and the OPEC+ meeting that's expected to bring another large output increase for September. A rash of U.S. trade deals, and threats of Russia sanctions have oil prices on track for solid weekly gains. If the sanctions are avoided, "if it's business as usual, the fundamentals for oil should weaken," says Neil Crosby, of Sparta Commodities. "A lot of people are expecting weaker demand going forward, OPEC are due to export probably a lot more oil after summer, and with those two things combined you should start to see more of these inventory builds." WTI settles down 1.1% at $69.26 a barrel. September Brent goes off the board at $72.53, down 1%. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1512 GMT - Shell is using its operational and financial strength to weather challenging macroeconomic conditions, Quilter Cheviot's Maurizio Carulli writes. While earnings in its key liquefied natural gas division dropped 30%, it won't be too much of a concern for investors, Carulli writes. The unit will bounce back once prices begin to recover, Carulli adds. Shares trade up 1.6% at 2,722 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1318 GMT - Oil futures are lower after a three-day rally driven by a spate of U.S. trade deals ahead of Friday's tariff deadline and threats of sanctions against Russia and buyers of its oil. If substantial tariffs are added to Russian oil buyers like India, the market reaction will "needless to say be hefty," Neil Crosby of Sparta Commodities says in a note. While chances are the threats aren't fully carried out, or not for long, replacing Russian crude would fall predominantly on the Dubai market, with more demand also for West African crude, WTI "and potentially some other European grades," he says. WTI is off 0.7% at $69.53 a barrel. September Brent is off 0.8% at $72.68. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0154 GMT - Productivity and simplification are key for Rio Tinto's next CEO, Simon Trott, to "return Rio to its former glory," Macquarie analysts say in a note. The analysts say Rio Tinto's minerals arm, which includes commodities such as titanium dioxide, borates and diamonds, undid all the good work of its copper division in 1H. "We think a portfolio simplification mantra could deliver value via reducing earnings volatility and risk." Macquarie raises its target on Rio's Australian shares by 3% to A$109/share. The stock is up 0.6% at A$112.32. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0143 GMT - A rebased commodity-price graph in Rio Tinto's 1H result shows copper and aluminum trading around long-term averages, but iron ore and lithium trading below. That prompts Macquarie analysts to contemplate the possible price risks it could face in those industries. "For those with long memories, they may recall when iron ore struggled to exceed $40/ton (nominal) prior to 2005 and lump VIU's [value-in-use] drove slim producer profits," say the analysts. At that time, the iron-ore market was experiencing oversupply from new basins, volume-based industry rivalry and strong customer buying power, they say. "We hope history does not repeat itself." (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0117 GMT - Efforts to better Mineral Resources' disclosure and capital allocation "have certainly improved MIN's investment proposition," Macquarie says in a note. The miner also appears poised to benefit from lower lithium production costs in the future, according to the bank. Macquarie raises its target on the stock by 32% to A$29/share. Yet, a 40% surge in the miner's shares since the start of July has already pushed its value past that target. Macquarie downgrades the stock to underperform from neutral, citing valuation and the potential for weaker iron-ore prices over the next couple of years. Mineral Resources is up 5.1% at A$30.04. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0053 GMT - IGO's lowered liquidity threshold, focus on productivity and planned impairment of the Kwinana refinery "all pave the way for a focus on shareholder returns while it navigates market volatility," Macquarie says. The bank raises its target on the stock by 11% to A$5.00/share. It reiterates an outperform rating. IGO's 4Q result is mixed, with a production miss offset by a sales beat, Macquarie says. IGO is up 1.7% at A$4.505, erasing a small part of this week's more-than-10% loss. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0033 GMT - Now could be a good time for Kingsgate Consolidated to sell its Nueva Esperanza silver project in Chile, Canaccord Genuity suggests. That's because silver prices have surged 30% so far this year. Nueva Esperanza has a resource of 134 million oz of silver, grading at 106 grams per ton of ore. "Should the project be divested, we think this could be a windfall and valuation catalyst for Kingsgate," analyst Reg Spencer says. It points to Andean Silver as a potential benchmark for valuing the project. Andean Silver, with a market value of A$232 million, owns the Cerro Bayo project in Chile that has a resource of 120 million oz of silver. "While Cerro Bayo is more advanced with established infrastructure, we think it could provide a valuation yardstick for any divestment by Kingsgate," Canaccord says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2256 GMT [Dow Jones]--Global softwood kraft pulp market conditions are expected to stay weak in the third quarter, Canfor Pulp Products said. "The subdued demand for bleached kraft paper in North America observed at the end of the first quarter and throughout the second quarter is anticipated to persist into the third quarter," Canfor Pulp said. The outlook is mostly attributed to ongoing uncertainties related to Canada-US trade relations, and general global economic pressures, the company added. Purchasing activity, especially from China, is expected to remain soft during the traditionally slower summer period, Canfor said, adding "as a result, global pulp producer inventories are forecast to remain well above the balanced range through the third quarter of 2025." (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0802 GMT - IAG's 250 million-euro EBIT beat in 2Q is enough to drive upgrades of 5% to 6%, RBC Capital Markets analyst Ruairi Cullinane says in a note. The airline group lowered its total fuel cost guidance for the year by 5.3% to 7.1 billion euros, which could make consensus estimates rise by even more, he says. The company is 57% booked for the second half with revenue in line with last year, compared with 29% booked for the 2H last year. "We think IAG has a stronger outlook than given credit for, given a supportive capacity backdrop on the Atlantic, pricing potential from BA product measures, and given scope for business travel recovery to step up further," he says. Shares are up 1.3% at 385.50 pence. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0732 GMT - Maruti Suzuki India appears optimistic on the demand outlook for 2Q FY 2026, Elara Securities (India) analysts say in a research report. The automaker sees demand picking up in 2Q, led by the early festival season, good monsoon and robust rural demand, the analysts note. The Indian company's two new launches within sports utility vehicles are on track, while dealer inventory is normalized at 33 days as of end-1Q. The key catalyst for the stock is a recovery in domestic demand in India, which will be keenly monitored by the brokerage during the festive season. The brokerage maintains the stock's accumulate rating, but raises the target price to INR14,279.00 from INR13,961.00 to reflect valuation roll-forward. Shares are 1.9% lower at INR12,393.90. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0703 GMT - IAG surpassed 2Q expectations with a 17% beat on operating profit, Bernstein analysts Alex Irving and Antoine Madre say on a note. Year-on-year passenger revenue per available seat-kilometer----a key revenue metric measuring the ratio between an airline's revenue and its passenger carrying capacity--grew 2.6% to 8.55 euros, they note. Still, the airline group's revenue seem close to last year's, which appears to imply some PRASK decline into 2H, albeit against tough comparisons, the analysts say. The company is likely to see changes to consensus earnings on these results, Bernstein says. Shares are up 2.5% at 390.10 pence. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0700 GMT - NIO's shares rose sharply in Hong Kong on Friday, thanks to positive initial feedback surrounding its new model, the Onvo L90, released late Thursday, Nomura's analyst Joel Ying said. Some analysts are comparing Onvo L90 with Li Auto's SUV model i8. Nomura thinks the Onvo L90 looks more attractive in terms of hardware configuration, but still needs to evaluate the market's trends. Morningstar's Vincent Sun thinks L90 is more attractive in terms of pricing, considering its large size and battery leasing option. Also, initial demand for Li Auto's i8 may not be spectacular when it launches at year end as it is priced about CNY300,000, Sun added. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0655 GMT - Asean market watchers will keep a close eye on sector-specific tariffs, RHB Bank group chief economist and head of market research Barnabas Gan says in a report. These tariffs pose downside risks for regional countries, especially those with export-heavy industries, Gan writes. "Malaysia and Singapore appear most exposed to potential semiconductor-related tariffs, while pharmaceutical-related measures would likely weigh heavily on Singapore and Thailand." Thailand's automotive sector and Indonesia's electric-vehicle battery segment could also be hurt by any new levies targeting the broader auto value chain, he says. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0654 GMT - Pirelli's cut to its full-year revenue guidance aligns the company's expectations with consensus estimates, Bernstein analysts say in a research note. The Italian tire maker now expects to generate revenue of 6.7 billion to 6.8 billion euros for the year, down from its previous range of 6.8 billion to 7 billion euros, citing foreign-exchange headwinds. Volume growth should accelerate in the second half and the effects of pricing and sales mix continue to help top-line growth, likely aided by some U.S. price increases, Bernstein says. "The impact of U.S. tariffs is as expected, and [the full-year] impact may even be slightly lower than embedded in the guide after the EU 15% tariff deal," the analysts say. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0555 GMT - Daimler Truck Holding cut its full-year expectations due to uncertainty clouding its North America business, and added a caveat to its outlook that suggests it remains at risk of further downgrades, Bernstein analysts say in a research note. The German truck and bus maker said its new guidance assumes the U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade agreement continues. However, a probe started by the U.S. government might change this, which would hurt Daimler, Bernstein say. The company lowered its adjusted operating profit expectations by 18% at the mid-point of its guidance range, and the mid-point of its new outlook sits 7% below consensus estimates, Bernstein says. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0516 GMT - Daimler Truck Holding's cut to its expectations for North America didn't come as a surprise, but the magnitude of the downgrade is likely to disappoint most and points to lower prices, Bernstein analysts say in a research note. "The extent of the cut also--worryingly--implies weakening pricing: the old industrial guide implied a revenue per unit of 111,000 euros, while the new guide implies 107,000 euros," the analysts say. The German truck and bus maker's guidance downgrade overshadows strong second-quarter results, with adjusted earnings topping consensus forecasts, Bernstein says. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0456 GMT - NIO's Onvo L90 seems to offer better value and user satisfaction than Li Auto's i8, Citi analysts say in a note. The Onvo L90 has a starting price of 265,800 yuan, compared with 321,800 yuan for the Li i8. Citi thinks the monthly run-rate for NIO's new car could reach 8,000 to 9,000 units, slightly ahead of market consensus, thanks to its attractive pricing and rich interior features. The L90 is now a clear standout among large battery-electric SUVs on the Chinese market, Citi says. It maintains a buy rating on NIO with a target price of $8.10. ADRs last closed at $4.87. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0158 GMT - HD Hyundai Heavy Industries could gain from a recovery in U.S. demand for liquefied natural gas carriers likely in late 2H of 2025 or early 1H of 2026, Daiwa Capital analysts Mike Oh and Daeho Son say. Final investment decisions on U.S. LNG projects are likely to proceed without major disruptions, the analysts write in a note. They raise their EPS forecasts for the South Korean shipbuilder by 29% each for 2025 and 2026 to reflect its backlog order mix that is improving at a faster-than-expected pace. The company's new contract wins so far this year have come in at $5.5 billion, about 70% of its $7.7 billion annual target, they note. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

1700 GMT - Research from North Dakota State University, done in partnership with the Department of Agriculture's Agricultural Marketing Service, shows that ports globally have adequate capacity for shipping wheat exports, but the ports of leading wheat exporters Russia and Ukraine have, at times, only limited shipping capacity depending on the time of year. But should Russia invest more into its shipping infrastructure, that could dramatically impact the amount of wheat that ships out of the U.S., says the study. "Expanding Russian port infrastructure in the Baltic and Caspian Sea regions results in a reduction of U.S. exports of between 550,000 to 875,000 metric tons per year, depending on the degree of expansion," the study says. CBOT wheat is down 0.9%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1336 GMT - Ferrari's top-line growth has slowed dramatically due to lower sales volumes, says Garrett Nelson, senior equity analyst at CFRA Research. The company now has its work cut out to hit the current EPS consensus of 9.05 euros for 2025, he notes. This is despite Ferrari's history of exceeding expectations, as evidenced by the fact its last quarterly EPS miss was five years ago, he adds. The company's second-quarter adjusted EPS of 2.38 euros was ahead of the 2.31 euros expected by consensus, while net revenue rose 4% to 1.79 billion euros--50 million euros short of consensus. Full-year revenue and adjusted EPS guidance was maintained for at least 7 billion euros and 8.60 euros, respectively. Shares fall 6.2%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Pakistan to buy first-ever US oil cargo in Cnergyico deal with Vitol
data.description:
Key points:
  • Pakistan's first US crude purchase after landmark trade deal
  • Deal diversifies crude sourcing, cuts reliance on Middle East
  • Cnergyico considering buying at least one US crude cargo a month
  • Company planning refinery upgrades, expects local demand growth

Pakistan's largest refiner Cnergyico will import 1 million barrels of oil from Vitol in October, Cnergyico's Vice Chairman Usama Qureshi said on Friday, the country's first-ever purchase of U.S. crude following a landmark trade deal.

The West Texas Intermediate light crude cargo will be loaded from Houston this month and is expected to arrive in Karachi in the second half of October, he said.

"This is a test spot cargo under our umbrella term agreement with Vitol. If it is commercially viable and available, we could import at least one cargo per month," Qureshi told Reuters, adding that Vitol was its long-term trading partner.

The deal follows months of negotiations that first began in April, he said, after U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to impose 29% tariffs on imports from Pakistan.

Qureshi said Pakistan's finance and petroleum ministries encouraged local refineries to explore U.S. crude imports after the April tariff announcement.

Vitol did not immediately respond to a request for comment sent outside of office hours.

On Thursday, Pakistan hailed a trade deal struck with the United States, its top export market, and said the agreement would increase investments. The White House said on Thursday the U.S. would charge a 19% tariff on imports from Pakistan.

A key China ally, Pakistan has been wooing Trump after he threatened tariffs. It credited U.S. diplomatic intervention for ending recent hostilities with neighbouring India and nominated Trump for the Nobel Peace Prize.

Oil is Pakistan's biggest import item and its shipments were valued at $11.3 billion in the year ended June 30, 2025, accounting for nearly a fifth of the country's total import bill.

The import deal will help Pakistan diversify its crude sourcing and reduce reliance on Middle Eastern suppliers, which account for nearly all of its oil imports.

"Gross refining margin is on par with Gulf grades, and no blending or refinery tweaks are required," Qureshi said.

Cnergyico could consider buying at least 1 million barrels of U.S. oil a month after it evaluates the first shipment, given its current monthly demand stands at 4.6 million barrels, he said.

"It aligns well with domestic market requirements. Demand typically strengthens in the October–November period," Qureshi said.

Cnergyico can process 156,000 barrels of crude per day and operates the country's only single-point mooring terminal near Karachi, enabling it to handle large tankers unlike other refiners in Pakistan.

The company plans to install a second offshore terminal to allow larger or more frequent shipments, and to upgrade its refinery over the next five to six years, Qureshi said.

The refiner, which has been operating at an average refinery run rate of 30% to 35% due to tepid local demand, is betting on growth in demand for oil products.

Trump said on Wednesday the U.S. would also cooperate with Pakistan to develop the South Asian country's "massive oil reserves", without providing further details.
